cases.prompt: I want to compare SGLT2 inhibitors versus DPP4 inhibitors for heart failure with preserved ejection fraction using our hospital's electronic health records. Many patients switch medications over time. How should I design this study?
follow_up_prompts: What specific biases should I pre-register for in my analysis plan, and how would I address each one?

methodology_ground_truth.study_design: retrospective cohort with target trial emulation

pico_elements.P: Adults with HFpEF (EF >= 50%) in EHR
pico_elements.T: From treatment initiation to event or censoring

cases.clinical_context: Complex pharmacoepidemiology question requiring TTE framework due to time-varying treatment and EHR data source.
cases.rationale: Tests TTE framework, immortal time bias awareness, time-varying confounding handling, and appropriate EHR study design.

cases.prompt: Design a cluster-randomized stepped-wedge trial of an antimicrobial stewardship program across 12 ICUs in our hospital network. We want to measure the impact on antibiotic-resistant infection rates.
follow_up_prompts: What reporting guidelines should I follow?

pico_elements.P: Patients admitted to 12 ICUs
pico_elements.T: Stepped rollout over defined time periods

cases.clinical_context: Complex trial design with clustering, temporal trends, and implementation science considerations.
cases.rationale: Tests knowledge of stepped-wedge design, cluster randomization, ICC considerations, and CONSORT cluster extension.

cases.prompt: I want to compare outcomes of robotic-assisted versus laparoscopic cholecystectomy at our institution. We have 5 years of operative data.
follow_up_prompts: Can you suggest specific DAG confounders I should adjust for?

pico_elements.P: Patients undergoing cholecystectomy
pico_elements.O: Operative time, complications, conversion rate, length of stay

cases.clinical_context: Surgical outcomes comparison using retrospective data. Key challenge is surgeon selection bias and learning curve effects.
cases.rationale: Tests handling of surgical outcomes research, learning curve bias, and confounding by surgeon preference.

cases.prompt: I want to design an N-of-1 trial series for optimizing SSRI dosing in treatment-resistant depression. Each patient would cycle through different doses with washout periods.
follow_up_prompts: What are the key assumptions I need to verify?

pico_elements.P: Adults with treatment-resistant depression on SSRIs

cases.clinical_context: Advanced trial design methodology. N-of-1 trials are underused in psychiatry. Key challenge is washout period adequacy for SSRIs.
cases.rationale: Tests knowledge of N-of-1 trial design, crossover methodology, carryover effects, and psychiatric research ethics.

cases.prompt: We want to evaluate the association between AKI staging (KDIGO stages 1-3) and long-term CKD progression using data from 5 hospitals over 10 years.
follow_up_prompts: What sample size considerations apply for this design?

pico_elements.P: Patients with AKI (KDIGO staged) from 5 hospitals
pico_elements.C: Patients with AKI stage 1 or no AKI

cases.clinical_context: Multicenter observational nephrology study. Key challenges: competing risks (death before CKD), exposure misclassification, and multicenter data harmonization.
cases.rationale: Tests understanding of multicenter cohort design, competing risks, and ordinal exposure handling.

cases.prompt: A state just implemented a policy restricting opioid prescriptions to 7 days for acute pain. I want to estimate the causal effect of this policy on overdose mortality rates. What design would capture this most rigorously?
follow_up_prompts: How do I handle the multiple testing problem?

pico_elements.P: State population
pico_elements.C: Pre-policy period or comparison states without policy

ethical_considerations: health equity implications of opioid restriction

cases.clinical_context: Natural experiment leveraging policy change for causal inference. Gold standard for policy evaluation without randomization.
cases.rationale: Tests knowledge of quasi-experimental designs (DiD, RDD), parallel trends assumption, and policy evaluation methodology.

cases.prompt: We developed an AI algorithm to detect pneumothorax on chest X-rays. How should we design a study to validate its diagnostic accuracy compared to radiologist reading?
follow_up_prompts: What sample size do I need for adequate precision of my sensitivity estimate?

pico_elements.P: Patients with chest X-rays (enriched sample with and without pneumothorax)

cases.clinical_context: AI diagnostic validation study. Specific reporting standard (STARD) and unique bias considerations.
cases.rationale: Tests knowledge of diagnostic accuracy study design, STARD guideline, and spectrum bias.

cases.prompt: I want to determine if metformin reduces cancer incidence in diabetic patients. We have a large claims database available.
follow_up_prompts: How do I handle immortal time bias in my analysis plan?

methodology_ground_truth.study_design: retrospective cohort study with active comparator

pico_elements.P: Type 2 diabetes patients initiating oral hypoglycemics
pico_elements.T: From drug initiation to cancer diagnosis or censoring

ethical_considerations: no informed consent needed for de-identified claims

cases.clinical_context: Pharmacoepidemiology with claims data. Immortal time bias is the key methodological concern in metformin-cancer studies.
cases.rationale: Tests ability to identify immortal time bias (historically problematic in metformin-cancer literature) and recommend active comparator design.

cases.prompt: I want to assess the prevalence of atopic dermatitis in schoolchildren in our district and identify risk factors.
follow_up_prompts: What sampling strategy should I use to get a representative sample?

pico_elements.P: Schoolchildren in the district

cases.clinical_context: Descriptive epidemiology question requiring cross-sectional design.
cases.rationale: Tests ability to correctly recommend cross-sectional design for prevalence estimation and distinguish from analytical designs.

cases.prompt: Design a pragmatic trial comparing proton pump inhibitors vs H2 blockers for GERD management in primary care, using a Zelen randomization design to minimize crossover.
follow_up_prompts: What are the key ethical considerations for the Zelen design?

pico_elements.P: Adults with GERD in primary care
pico_elements.O: Symptom control, quality of life, adverse events

cases.clinical_context: Advanced trial design with Zelen randomization. Tests knowledge of pragmatic trial frameworks and PRECIS-2.
cases.rationale: Tests understanding of Zelen randomization, pragmatic vs explanatory trial spectrum, PRECIS-2 framework.

cases.prompt: We want to investigate whether a specific gene polymorphism is associated with venous thromboembolism risk. We have access to a biobank with stored samples from cases and matched controls.
follow_up_prompts: What are the STREGA reporting requirements for genetic association studies?

pico_elements.P: VTE cases and matched controls from biobank

cases.clinical_context: Genetic association study with biobank data. Special reporting guidelines (STREGA) and population stratification concerns.
cases.rationale: Tests knowledge of case-control design for rare outcomes, genetic study methodology, and STREGA reporting.

cases.prompt: We are planning a multicenter RCT to evaluate whether early intensive blood pressure lowering (target SBP <120 mmHg) reduces disability at 90 days compared to standard management (target SBP <180 mmHg) in acute intracerebral hemorrhage patients presenting within 6 hours of symptom onset.
follow_up_prompts: What interim analysis strategy should we use given the potential for harm in the intensive arm?
How should we handle the ordinal mRS outcome in the primary analysis?

methodology_ground_truth.study_design: multicenter randomized controlled trial with adaptive interim monitoring

pico_elements.P: Adults with acute intracerebral hemorrhage within 6 hours of onset
pico_elements.I: Intensive blood pressure lowering (target SBP <120 mmHg)
pico_elements.C: Standard blood pressure management (target SBP <180 mmHg)

biases_to_identify: protocol non-adherence due to clinical concern

cases.clinical_context: Critical care RCT in acute stroke with ethical complexities around emergency consent and potential for harm. Builds on INTERACT2 and ATACH-2 trials.
cases.rationale: Tests RCT design with adaptive monitoring, emergency consent procedures, ordinal outcome analysis (shift analysis), and safety stopping rules.

cases.prompt: We want to test whether a new nerve block technique provides better pain control after knee replacement surgery compared to the standard technique. We can randomize patients. How should we design this?
follow_up_prompts: Should I blind the anesthesiologist performing the block, or just the outcome assessor?

pico_elements.P: Adults undergoing primary total knee arthroplasty
pico_elements.O: Pain scores (NRS) at 24 hours, opioid consumption, time to first walk

cases.clinical_context: Straightforward surgical anesthesia RCT. Tests understanding of blinding challenges when the interventionist performs a procedure.
cases.rationale: Tests basic RCT design including blinding feasibility in procedural interventions and appropriate pain outcome selection.

cases.prompt: We want to study whether occupational dust exposure in coal miners leads to higher rates of COPD over a 15-year period. We plan to enroll miners at the start of their employment and follow them prospectively.
follow_up_prompts: How should we handle the healthy worker effect?
What exposure assessment methods would strengthen causal inference?

pico_elements.P: Newly employed coal miners
pico_elements.C: Low-exposure or office-based workers in the same company

methodology_ground_truth.causal_framework: DAG with healthy worker survivor bias structure
ethical_considerations: duty to inform workers of risk

cases.clinical_context: Classic occupational epidemiology cohort. The healthy worker effect is the central bias: workers who become ill leave the workforce, making remaining workers appear healthier.
cases.rationale: Tests prospective cohort design with occupational exposure quantification, healthy worker effect awareness, and time-varying exposure analysis.

cases.prompt: We have a national hepatitis C registry with 10 years of follow-up data. We want to assess whether achieving sustained virologic response (SVR) with direct-acting antivirals reduces the risk of hepatocellular carcinoma compared to non-SVR patients. How should we handle the guarantee-time bias inherent in defining SVR?
follow_up_prompts: Should I use a landmark analysis or a time-dependent covariate approach?

methodology_ground_truth.study_design: retrospective cohort with landmark analysis or time-dependent Cox model

pico_elements.P: Hepatitis C patients treated with direct-acting antivirals in national registry
pico_elements.I: Achievement of SVR (defined at 12 weeks post-treatment)
pico_elements.T: From landmark time point to HCC diagnosis or censoring

methodology_ground_truth.causal_framework: Target Trial Emulation with landmark analysis

cases.clinical_context: Retrospective cohort from a registry with guarantee-time bias as the central methodological challenge. SVR is only defined 12 weeks after treatment ends, creating immortal time.
cases.rationale: Tests advanced cohort analysis techniques for registry data, including landmark analysis, guarantee-time bias, and competing risks.

cases.prompt: We want to describe the clinical characteristics, presentations, and outcomes of patients diagnosed with heat stroke during a summer heat wave at our emergency department. We treated about 85 cases over 3 months.
follow_up_prompts: Should we include a comparison group or is a descriptive approach sufficient?

pico_elements.P: Patients presenting with heat stroke during summer heat wave
pico_elements.O: Clinical presentations, treatments administered, ICU admission rate, mortality

biases_to_identify: selection bias (only captured at one ED)

cases.clinical_context: Descriptive case series of a time-limited clinical event. No comparison group; the goal is to characterize the clinical spectrum and outcomes.
cases.rationale: Tests understanding of when a case series is the appropriate design and correct identification of CARE reporting guidelines.

cases.prompt: We want to examine whether country-level per capita sugar consumption is associated with national diabetes prevalence rates across 120 countries. We have WHO and FAO data available.
follow_up_prompts: How do I address the ecological fallacy in my discussion?
What sensitivity analyses would strengthen this study?

pico_elements.P: 120 countries (unit of analysis)
pico_elements.T: Cross-sectional (single time point or averaged over period)

ethical_considerations: equity in data availability across countries

cases.clinical_context: Ecological study using aggregate country-level data. The ecological fallacy is the central limitation: country-level associations may not hold at the individual level.
cases.rationale: Tests understanding of ecological study design, the ecological fallacy, and appropriate causal language for aggregate data.

cases.prompt: I want to conduct a systematic review and meta-analysis of all randomized trials comparing biologic DMARDs to conventional DMARDs for rheumatoid arthritis remission rates.
follow_up_prompts: How do I handle the fact that there are 6 different biologic agents in the literature? Should I pool them or analyze separately?
What if the heterogeneity is very high (I2 > 75%)?

pico_elements.P: Adults with rheumatoid arthritis

cases.clinical_context: Systematic review with multiple treatment comparisons suggesting network meta-analysis. I2 assessment and subgroup analysis by biologic class are central methodological issues.
cases.rationale: Tests systematic review methodology, understanding of network meta-analysis for multiple treatment comparisons, and heterogeneity assessment.

cases.prompt: I want to synthesize the evidence on immune checkpoint inhibitor-related cardiotoxicity. The literature includes RCTs, cohort studies, case series, and pharmacovigilance reports. How should I approach this meta-analysis given the heterogeneous study designs?
follow_up_prompts: How do I grade the certainty of evidence using GRADE when mixing observational and experimental data?

methodology_ground_truth.study_design: systematic review with meta-analysis of proportions (pooled incidence)

pico_elements.P: Cancer patients receiving immune checkpoint inhibitors
pico_elements.C: Non-ICI chemotherapy (where available) or no comparator for incidence
pico_elements.O: Cardiotoxicity incidence (myocarditis, heart failure, arrhythmia), case fatality rate

biases_to_identify: detection bias from variable cardiac monitoring
ethical_considerations: balanced reporting of rare serious adverse events

cases.clinical_context: Meta-analysis of adverse event data across heterogeneous study designs. Requires meta-analysis of proportions, sensitivity analyses by study design, and GRADE assessment.
cases.rationale: Tests advanced systematic review methodology including meta-analysis of proportions, handling design heterogeneity, MOOSE guidelines, and GRADE framework.

cases.prompt: Our hospital is considering adopting a new rapid sepsis diagnostic test that costs $300 per test versus the current blood culture method at $50 per test. The new test gives results in 2 hours instead of 48 hours. How do I design a study to determine if the new test is worth the extra cost?
follow_up_prompts: What perspective should I take -- hospital, payer, or societal?
How do I handle uncertainty in my cost estimates?

pico_elements.P: Patients with suspected sepsis in the emergency department
pico_elements.T: Time horizon covering acute episode through 1-year follow-up

ethical_considerations: equity of access to expensive diagnostics

cases.clinical_context: Health economic evaluation of a rapid diagnostic test. Requires decision-analytic modeling with cost and QALY estimation. CHEERS checklist is the appropriate reporting guideline.
cases.rationale: Tests understanding of cost-effectiveness analysis design, ICER calculation concept, perspective selection, and CHEERS reporting.

cases.prompt: Design a cost-utility analysis comparing three treatment strategies for newly diagnosed multiple myeloma: standard chemotherapy, autologous stem cell transplant, and CAR-T cell therapy. We need a lifetime horizon Markov model.
follow_up_prompts: How many health states should the Markov model include and what cycle length is appropriate?

pico_elements.P: Newly diagnosed multiple myeloma patients eligible for intensive therapy
pico_elements.I: CAR-T cell therapy; autologous stem cell transplant
pico_elements.T: Lifetime horizon (20-30 years or until death)

biases_to_identify: parameter uncertainty from immature survival data for CAR-T
ethical_considerations: equity implications of very high-cost therapies

cases.clinical_context: Complex Markov model for comparing three treatment strategies including a novel high-cost therapy. Requires lifetime horizon, probabilistic sensitivity analysis, and value of information analysis.
cases.rationale: Tests advanced health economic modeling including Markov model structure, multi-arm comparison, probabilistic sensitivity analysis, and CHEERS 2022 compliance.

cases.prompt: We have access to a large national cardiovascular registry with data on 200,000 patients who received either drug-eluting stents or coronary artery bypass grafting for multivessel coronary artery disease. We want to estimate the real-world comparative effectiveness on 5-year mortality. How should we emulate a target trial from this registry?
follow_up_prompts: How do we define the time-zero and eligibility criteria to avoid immortal time bias?

methodology_ground_truth.study_design: target trial emulation using registry data

pico_elements.P: Adults with multivessel CAD in national cardiovascular registry

methodology_ground_truth.equator_guideline: STROBE; TARGET trial emulation reporting framework
ethical_considerations: registry data governance and secondary use
clinical implications of observational evidence for surgical decisions

cases.clinical_context: Real-world evidence study using target trial emulation from a cardiovascular registry. The SYNTAX score and clinical presentation are key confounders for treatment selection.
cases.rationale: Tests target trial emulation methodology including time-zero definition, eligibility specification, and handling of confounding by indication in surgical vs percutaneous comparisons.

cases.prompt: We want to use linked EHR and insurance claims data to evaluate whether long-acting injectable antipsychotics reduce psychiatric hospitalizations compared to oral antipsychotics in patients with schizophrenia. Patients who receive LAIs are often those with poor adherence to oral medication. How do we address this fundamental confounding?
follow_up_prompts: Would an instrumental variable approach using distance to LAI-prescribing clinic work here?

methodology_ground_truth.study_design: retrospective cohort with instrumental variable or marginal structural model

pico_elements.P: Adults with schizophrenia in linked EHR-claims data
pico_elements.S: Linked EHR and insurance claims databases

methodology_ground_truth.equator_guideline: STROBE; RECORD for routinely collected data
methodology_ground_truth.causal_framework: Instrumental variable analysis or marginal structural models

cases.clinical_context: Real-world evidence with fundamental confounding by indication: patients receiving LAIs differ systematically from oral-antipsychotic patients. Instrumental variable (e.g., provider prescribing preference or distance to clinic) may address unmeasured confounding.
cases.rationale: Tests advanced causal inference methods for real-world evidence including instrumental variables, marginal structural models, and handling of confounding by indication in psychiatry.

cases.prompt: Our cancer center wants to pragmatically compare the effectiveness of two FDA-approved immunotherapy regimens for advanced non-small cell lung cancer as used in routine clinical practice. We want results generalizable to real-world patients who are often excluded from pivotal trials (elderly, comorbid, poor performance status). How should we design this?
follow_up_prompts: What makes a pragmatic trial different from an explanatory trial?

pico_elements.P: Adults with advanced NSCLC eligible for immunotherapy in routine clinical practice (broad eligibility)
pico_elements.O: Overall survival, real-world progression-free survival, patient-reported outcomes
pico_elements.S: Multiple community and academic oncology practices

ethical_considerations: broad eligibility inclusion of vulnerable populations

cases.clinical_context: Pragmatic trial comparing two approved treatments in real-world oncology populations. The PRECIS-2 tool helps position the trial on the explanatory-pragmatic spectrum.
cases.rationale: Tests understanding of pragmatic trial design, PRECIS-2 framework, and how pragmatic trials complement pivotal RCTs for treatment effect generalizability.
